paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1184 English Language — AI-authored Simplified Practice 22: The Frog at MacRitchie (§B style, simplified band)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary 1 girl on a school excursion to the MacRitchie Treetop Walk who finds a tiny green frog on the suspension bridge, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [15 marks] — AI-authored simplified practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1184/02 (formerly 1128) Section B format and calibrated to the SEAB assessment objectives (literal / inferential comprehension, vocabulary in context, language for effect), pitched at students stretching from middle secondary toward the full O-Level standard. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. Items mix mark values [1]/[2]: 7 short-answer questions (Q1-Q7) — 1 literal, 2 vocabulary, 3 inference and 1 language-for-effect — plus a 4-blank emotion-flowchart MCQ (Q8). Answer in your own words as far as possible.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
On a Tuesday in March, our Sec 1 class went to MacRitchie Reservoir for Geography fieldwork. Before the walk, Mrs Tan, our form teacher, gathered us under a Tembusu tree and reminded us that the forest was a home. 'This is the frog's house,' she said, 'not ours. The trees, the lizards, the spiders — all of this is theirs. We are only visitors today.'

Paragraph 2
My two best friends, Priya and Yi Xin, walked on either side of me. We had been put into groups of three. By the time we reached the suspension bridge of the Treetop Walk, my water bottle was almost empty. The bridge swayed a little.

Paragraph 3
Halfway across, Priya stopped so suddenly that Yi Xin bumped into her back. Priya pointed at the wooden railing. There, sitting completely still, was the tiniest frog I had ever seen. It was bright green, no bigger than my thumbnail. Its eyes were like two small black dots. It seemed to think that if it stayed very still, we would not see it.

Paragraph 4
Yi Xin pulled out her empty water bottle. 'We can put it inside,' she whispered. 'Take it back to school.' Priya nodded. She had already cupped her hand near the frog, ready to scoop it up. The frog still did not move.

Paragraph 5
I did not say anything. I thought about Mrs Tan under the Tembusu tree. 'This is the frog's house,' she had said, 'not ours.' I looked at the bottle in Yi Xin's hand. I looked at the frog. I looked at the long way back to the bus.

Paragraph 6
'No,' I said. My voice was small but clear. 'We can't take it. Mrs Tan said this is the frog's house. If we put it in the bottle, we are stealing from its house.' Yi Xin made a face. Priya rolled her eyes. 'Aiyoh, Ling, it's just one frog,' she said. But she did not move her hand any closer.

Paragraph 7
Nobody spoke. Then Yi Xin slowly put the water bottle back into her bag. Priya found a wide green leaf on the railing and gently nudged the frog onto it. The frog blinked once and hopped into the bushes below. None of us spoke after that.

Paragraph 8
On the bus back to school, the air conditioning hummed and most of the class fell asleep. Yi Xin sat next to me, looking out of the window. Without turning her head, she said quietly, 'Ling. You were right about the frog.' I did not say anything. I leaned my forehead against the cool glass and looked at the trees passing by.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1184 §B style practice, simplified-band difficulty; not from a past examination paper. MacRitchie: a large reservoir and nature reserve in central Singapore; Treetop Walk: a free-standing suspension bridge through the forest canopy; Tembusu: a large native Singaporean tree; aiyoh: a Singlish exclamation expressing mild frustration.)

questions.stem: Q1. From Paragraph 1, what did Mrs Tan tell the class they were in the forest that day? [1]
Q2. What does the word 'gathered' (Paragraph 1) suggest about how Mrs Tan got the class together? [1]
questions.answer: she brought them all into one group / close together

questions.stem: Q3. What does the phrase 'no bigger than my thumbnail' (Paragraph 3) suggest about the frog? [1]
Q4. Using your own words, explain why the narrator looked 'at the long way back to the bus' before she spoke. (Paragraph 5) [2]
questions.answer: ① she was thinking how far they would have to carry the frog; ② she realised keeping it in a bottle for that long trip would be unkind to such a small creature

questions.stem: Q5. Using your own words, explain what Priya's action of not moving 'her hand any closer' (Paragraph 6) shows about how she really felt. [2]
questions.answer: ① even though she pretended to disagree, she had stopped trying to catch the frog; ② this shows that deep down she knew the narrator was right

questions.stem: Q6. Using your own words, explain how Yi Xin had changed by the time she spoke on the bus, 'Ling. You were right about the frog' (Paragraph 8). [2]
questions.answer: ① at first she wanted to keep the frog in the bottle; ② now she quietly admits the narrator was correct to let it go

questions.stem: Q7. What is the effect of describing the frog's eyes as 'like two small black dots' (Paragraph 3)? [2]
questions.answer: the simile makes the frog seem tiny and delicate, almost like a toy, so the reader sees how harmless and vulnerable it is

sections.instruction: Q8. The narrator's main feeling shifts as the excursion at MacRitchie unfolds. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best describes her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: curious / torn / resolute / quietly proud / bored / scared / annoyed / surprised. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Frog at MacRitchie' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift through the afternoon — from curious as she crouches down to look at the tiny green frog on the railing, to torn as Yi Xin pulls out the empty water bottle and Priya cups her hand to scoop it up, to resolute when she says clearly that they cannot take it, ending quietly proud on the bus ride home when Yi Xin tells her she was right. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q8(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 3 as she crouches down to look at the bright green frog sitting motionless on the wooden railing? [1]

Q8(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 4-5 as Yi Xin pulls out the empty water bottle, Priya cups her hand near the frog, and the narrator thinks back to Mrs Tan under the Tembusu tree? [1]
Q8(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 6 as she says, in a small but clear voice, 'No. We can't take it. Mrs Tan said this is the frog's house'? [1]

Q8(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 8 on the bus ride back to school, leaning her forehead against the cool glass after Yi Xin quietly tells her she was right about the frog? [1]
